edges.description: CD8 T-cell- and histiocyte-driven apoptosis produces the paracortical karyorrhectic necrosis that manifests clinically as self-limited lymphadenitis.
edges.source: CD8 T-Cell- and Histiocyte-Mediated Apoptotic Necrosis
edges.target: Paracortical Necrosis with Karyorrhexis and Self-Limited Lymphadenitis

nodes.description: In KFD lesions, an exaggerated cell-mediated immune response produces apoptotic cell death driven predominantly by CD8-positive cytotoxic T cells, acting through the Fas-Fas ligand and perforin/granzyme pathways. Histiocytes (macrophages) act as enhancers and phagocytose the apoptotic debris, generating paracortical foci of necrosis rich in karyorrhectic (nuclear) debris characteristically without neutrophils.
nodes.id: CD8 T-Cell- and Histiocyte-Mediated Apoptotic Necrosis

evidence.reference_title: Perforin and Fas pathways of cytotoxic T-cells in histiocytic necrotizing lymphadenitis.
evidence.snippet: Two molecular mechanisms of T-cell-mediated cytotoxicity, one perforin-based and the other Fas-based, have been demonstrated, and both systems induce apoptosis of the target cells.

evidence.reference_title: Perforin and Fas pathways of cytotoxic T-cells in histiocytic necrotizing lymphadenitis.
evidence.snippet: the apoptotic cells were T-cells, especially CD8-positive cells rather than CD4-positive cells

nodes.description: The converging cytotoxic and histiocytic response produces the clinical hallmark: subacute, often tender regional (typically cervical) lymphadenopathy with fever, arising from paracortical necrosis rich in apoptotic karyorrhectic debris. The process is self-limited and usually resolves spontaneously over weeks to months.
nodes.id: Paracortical Necrosis with Karyorrhexis and Self-Limited Lymphadenitis

evidence.reference_title: Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease: investigating comprehensive clinicopathological features and risk factors for recurrence.
evidence.snippet: Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease (KFD) is a rare disease that typically manifests with fever and cervical lymphadenopathy.
Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease typically follows a self-limiting course lasting several months
